title-group.article-title: Optimization of mix proportions and hydration study of high-strength concrete incorporating corn stalk ash and silica fume as supplementary cementitious materials

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/01khf5d59grid.412616.60000 0001 0002 2355School of Architecture and Civil Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006 China
2https://ror.org/01n2bd587grid.464369.a0000 0001 1122 661XSchool of Civil Engineering, Liaoning Technical University, Fuxin, 123000 China
3https://ror.org/0515nd386grid.412243.20000 0004 1760 1136School of Horticulture, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, 150030 China

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: To mitigate environmental pollution caused by the indiscriminate disposal of corn stalks and cement production, this study utilized corn stalk ash (CSA) and silica fume (SF) as partial replacements for cementitious materials in the formulation of biomass ash high-strength concrete. The investigation focused on the effects of three key variables—CSA content, water-to-binder ratio (W/B), and sand-to-binder ratio (S/B)—on the compressive strength of corn stalk high-strength concrete(CSHC). Using an orthogonal experimental design, the analysis determined that the factors influenced the 28-day compressive strength in the following order: S/B > CSA content > W/B.The highest 28-day compressive strength of CHSC, measured at 109.7 MPa, was achieved under the conditions of 15% CSA content, a W/B ratio of 0.21, and an S/B ratio of 1.2. Characterization of hydration products in the highest-strength samples at 7 and 28 days was performed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and scanning electron microscopy coupled with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (SEM–EDS). The results revealed that the abundant reactive SiO2 in CSA significantly promotes the secondary hydration of cement, leading to increased formation of calcium silicate hydrate (C–S–H) and thereby enhancing the mechanical strength of CSHC. This research provides a theoretical foundation supporting the engineering application of CSA as a supplementary cementitious material in the production of high-strength biomass ash concrete.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In response to the increasing demand for construction in society, high-strength concrete has gained significant prominence. Concrete is the second most widely consumed material globally, after water, with an annual usage approaching three billion tons. Research aimed at enhancing the sustainability of concrete is essential for mitigating its environmental impact1–3.Cement, a primary component of concrete, is associated with substantial carbon dioxide emissions; approximately one ton of CO2 is emitted per ton of cement produced. This production process accounts for 5 to 7% of anthropogenic global carbon emissions and 2% to 3% of the world’s total energy consumption. The use of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) as partial replacements for cement in concrete manufacturing is a key strategy for reducing carbon emissions related to cement production4 .Consequently, various industrial and agricultural solid wastes have attracted considerable research interest as potential SCMs5–8.

Annually, substantial quantities of agricultural solid wastes—including rice husks, bagasse9, and corn cobs10–15—as well as industrial solid wastes such as slag and silica fume16-17, are generated. Improper management of agricultural solid waste, particularly through unregulated burning and landfilling, significantly contributes to increased carbon emissions and the release of other harmful gases, resulting in severe contamination of air, soil, and groundwater resources18–20 .The incorporation of agricultural solid waste into concrete has been extensively studied and is currently practiced. These wastes can serve as supplementary cementitious materials or as aggregates within concrete mixtures, thereby maintaining or enhancing concrete performance while promoting environmental sustainability21-22. For example, materials such as rice husk ash, palm oil fuel ash, bagasse ash, corn cobs, corn stalks, and coconut shells have been used to partially replace cement or aggregates, providing dual benefits for both ecological preservation and engineering applications23-24. The primary rationale for using biomass ash as a partial cement replacement lies in its high content of reactive silica, which exhibits strong pozzolanic activity. This reactive silica participates in secondary hydration reactions with Ca(OH)₂ produced during cement hydration, forming calcium silicate hydrate that effectively fills the pores between cement particles. As a result, this process densifies the concrete matrix, enhancing its mechanical strength and durability25.

The utilization of corn stalk ash (CSA) has attracted considerable interest within the research community26–31. Optimal calcination of CSA occurs at temperatures between 500 and 600 °C. Specifically, calcination at 500 °C yields pozzolanic activity of up to 96.8%, while calcining at 600 °C for two hours effectively inhibits the crystallization of SiO₂, preserving approximately 92.5% of its pozzolanic activity30-32. Increasing the CSA replacement ratio corresponds with a reduction in concrete workability, with more pronounced declines observed at higher substitution levels. However, the addition of 0.25% water-reducing agent significantly enhances the workability of the concrete mixture33-34. Empirical evidence indicates that substituting 8% of the cement mass with CSA results in a 4.0% increase in 28-day compressive strength compared to the control, achieving a strength of 20.8 MPa. Conversely, increasing the CSA replacement to 10% leads to a decrease in compressive strength35–37, the compressive strength of the concrete decreases due to the agglomeration effect of corn stalk ash. The elastic modulus follows a similar trend to that of compressive strength. Furthermore, hydrophobic treatment of CSA using linseed oil has been shown to improve both the density and thermal conductivity of CSA-incorporated concrete38. The combined use of sunflower core ash with a binary replacement of 20% CSA and 4% other materials facilitates the production of ultra-high-performance concrete, yielding an 18.5% reduction in costs. Regarding durability, a composite replacement strategy involving 30% sunflower core ash (SBA) and 8% CSA promotes the formation of calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) gel through particle packing and pozzolanic reactions, thereby optimizing the concrete’s microstructure and reducing permeability39. Simultaneously, substituting cement with CSA and SF in concrete manufacturing can lower production expenses by 20%40.Notably, existing literature has yet to explore the relationship between varying CSA replacement rates and other factors influencing concrete strength, nor has it provided a detailed analysis of the resulting hydration products.However, no researchers have examined the connection between the CSA replacement rate and other factors influencing concrete strength, nor have they analyzed the hydration products.

This research increased the proportion of CSA and further clarified its substitution rate, along with the combined effects of other factors influencing concrete strength. By examining the hydration products and their interactions, a more efficient and optimal mix design can be developed. This paper employs orthogonal analysis to rank the impact of various factors on CHSC and correlates these results with microscopic analysis, providing valuable insights for other researchers. This method not only facilitates the creation of stronger concrete but also supports the advancement of sustainable construction practices.

The primary aim of this research is to examine the influence of the CSA quality replacement rate, water-to-binder(W/B) ratio, and sand-to-binder(S/B) ratio on the mechanical properties and microstructural characteristics of CHSC. Microstructural analyses were conducted using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), while mechanical properties were systematically evaluated to achieve the study’s objectives.

sec.title: Materials
sec.p: The cement utilized in this research is Ordinary Portland cement (P.O. 52.5), manufactured by Yatai Group Harbin Cement Co., Ltd. The characteristics, as supplied by the manufacturer, are detailed in Table 1.

Corn stalk samples were obtained from the Heilongjiang region in China and subsequently subjected to thermal treatment at 500 °C30-32 by Shengxiang Activated Carbon Company. The specific surface area of the resulting corn stalk ash (CSA) was measured using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method, with results depicted in Fig. 1, indicating a surface area of 18.69 m²/g. The microstructural characteristics of CSA were examined via scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the images of which are also presented in Fig. 1. Furthermore, the chemical composition of CSA was determined through X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysis, with the findings summarized in Table 2.

Silica fume(SF) used in this study was supplied by Gongyi Longze Water Purification Materials Co., Ltd., as detailed in Table 3. The characteristics of the silica fume comply with the specifications outlined in the GB/T 27,690 standard.

In this study, both fine and coarse aggregates were sourced from Qiqihar, Heilongjiang. The fine aggregate consisted of conventional river sand, prepared by sieving, with a moisture content of 4%, a bulk density of 1850 kg/m³, and a fineness modulus of 2.3. The coarse aggregate was commercially obtained crushed stone, characterized by a moisture content of 2%, a bulk density of 1600 kg/m³, and a compressive strength of 158 MPa. The particle size distribution of these aggregates is shown in Fig. 2.

The steel fibers used in this study consist of straight, brass-coated steel fibers. Previous research has demonstrated that incorporating steel fibers promotes a shift in the failure mode of high-strength concrete from brittle to ductile. Accordingly, a volume fraction of 2% steel fibers was employed in preparing the CHSC in the present investigation41. The detailed physical and mechanical properties of the fibers are presented in Table 4.

The superplasticizer used in this study is a commercially available, polycarboxylate-based, high-efficiency water-reducing agent that complies with the GB50119 standard. It is produced by Shanxi Feike New Material Technology Co., Ltd., China, and demonstrates a water reduction capacity of 25%.

caption.p: Chemical composition and elements of CSA(%).

sec.title: Preparation of test pieces
sec.p: Accurately weigh the specified quantities of test materials, then sequentially add cement, silica fume, and corn stalk ash into a pre-moistened cement mortar mixer. Blend the dry powders at medium-low speed for 30 s to ensure thorough and homogeneous mixing. Next, add water along with SP and continue mixing at low speed for 60 s, followed by high-speed mixing for 240 s. Incorporate the aggregate into the mixer and mix at low speed for 60 s. Finally, introduce steel fibers and mix for 2 min to achieve uniform dispersion. Transfer the resulting mixture into triple molds measuring 100 × 100 × 100 mm³. After curing the specimens under standard conditions (temperature maintained at 20 ± 1 °C and relative humidity above 95%) for 24 h, demold the samples. Conduct Unconfined Compressive Strength (UCS) tests after curing periods of 7 and 28 days, calculating the mean value from three specimens per group; exclude specimens with an error margin exceeding 15%. The procedure are illustrated in Fig. 3.

caption.p: Sample preparation and testing flow chart.

sec.title: Testing instruments
sec.p: The specific surface area of CSA was measured using the BET method with a Gemini 2390 instrument (Micromeritics, USA). The chemical composition of the CSA was characterized by X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XRF) using a PANalytical Zetium spectrometer (UK). Mineral composition analysis was performed via X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Smart Lab SE diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Additionally, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations were conducted using a Guoyi Quantum DB500 SEM instrument (China).

sec.title: Testing methods
sec.p: The unconfined compressive strength (UCS) test was conducted in accordance with the guidelines specified in the “Standard Test Method for Mechanical Properties of Ordinary Concrete” (GB/T 50081)42. Upon reaching the designated curing age, the samples were immersed in an ethanol solution to halt the hydration process, then dried and subjected to X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. Before further examination, the dried specimens were coated with a thin layer of gold. For scanning electron microscopy coupled with energy-dispersive spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) analysis, specimen dimensions ranged from 0.5 to 1 cm.

caption.p: Orthogonal design table for CHSC mix proportions.

table-wrap-foot.p: aThe combined substitution rate of CSA and SF was set at 20%.

sec.title: Mix design
sec.p: CHSC was produced by incorporating CSA and SF as SCMs, sand, aggregates, steel fibers, and SP. The combined substitution rate of CSA and SF was set at 20%. Variations in the mixture were analyzed by adjusting the CSA mass proportion, modifying the sand/binder(S/B) ratio, and the water/binder(W/B) ratio. The formulation was developed using a three-factor four-level orthogonal array L16 (4^3). The compressive strength of the cement materials at 7 and 28 days was utilized as the assessment criterion. In the conducted experiment, the mass fractions of CSA (A), the sand/binder ratio (B), and the water/binder ratio (C) were used as independent variables within a three-factor, four-level orthogonal experimental design. Notably, the combined content of CSA and silica fume (SF) constituted 20% of the total cementitious material.Table 5 displays the specific values and levels of each factor in the orthogonal array L16 (4^3). The total aggregate content was kept constant at 2.3 times that of the cementitious material, while the steel fibers were added at a fixed rate of 2% of the volume dosage.The amount of SP added is 4% of the cementitious material.

sec.title: Compressive strength
sec.p: Figure 4 illustrates the progression of compressive strength in CHSC influenced by three factors: CSA content, S/B, and W/B. The C12 specimen, featuring a 15% CSA mass substitution rate, a S/B ratio of 1.2, and a W/B ratio of 0.21, achieved a peak 28-day strength of 109.7 MPa, with a corresponding 7-day strength of 75.1 MPa. Six concrete mixtures exhibited a 28-day compressive strength exceeding 80 MPa, suggesting the viability of utilizing CSA as a cementitious material to partially replace cement in high-strength concrete production.

Figure 4 illustrates that the compressive strength of the samples at both 7 and 28 days exhibits a response with increasing CSA content, initially decreasing, then increasing, followed by a subsequent decline. Similarly, variations in the W/B and S/B ratios reveal a trend in which the 7-day and 28-day UCS values first decrease and then increase. When the CSA content is maintained at 15%, the 28-day compressive strength tends to rise with increasing W/B and S/B ratios, reaching a maximum value of 109.7 MPa. Conversely, at a CSA content of 5% the 7-day compressive strength demonstrates a fluctuating pattern—decreasing, then increasing, and decreasing again—as the W/B and S/B ratios increase, with a peak strength of 79.1 MPa observed at a W/B ratio of 0.18 and a S/B ratio of 0.9. At a CSA content of 20%, corresponding to the absence of silica fume, the overall concrete strength is comparatively lower. Under these conditions, the combination of the highest S/B ratio (1.2) and the lowest W/B ratio (0.18) results in the lowest 28-day compressive strength of 67.2 MPa, representing only 61.3% of the peak strength. Based on the maximum 28-day compressive strength achieved, the optimal mix proportions are identified as a CSA content of 15%, a W/B ratio of 0.21, and a S/B ratio of 1.2.

An analysis of the factors influencing the 28-day compressive strength reveals that the observed reduction in compressive strength with increasing W/B ratio deviates from trends reported in most prior studies. This discrepancy is attributed to the distinctive physical characteristics and microstructural features of the auxiliary cementitious material (CSA) used in this investigation. Notably, CSA exhibits a larger specific surface area and a porous particle surface morphology. The specific surface area of CSA is measured at 18.69 m²/g, approximately 1.15 times greater than that of silica fume (SF). Consequently, achieving satisfactory workability requires a higher water content. Furthermore, CSA contains a substantial amount of SiO2. When sufficient space is available for hydration, the free SiO243–47 can participate in a secondary hydration reaction with Ca(OH)2 produced during the primary hydration process, resulting in the formation of additional calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) products. This secondary reaction contributes to the enhancement of the mixture’s 28-day compressive strength.

When the CSA content is maintained at 5%, a lower W/B ratio correlates with increased 7-day compressive strength. This observation suggests that at reduced W/B ratios, the limited space restricts the effectiveness of the secondary hydration reaction. Nevertheless, the lower W/B ratio produces a relatively dense matrix, which accounts for the superior early-age strength. The incorporation of silica fume generates a synergistic interaction with the volcanic ash derived from corn stalks, resulting in an improvement in the compressive strength of the concrete.Additionally, the incorporation of steel fibers acts as a reinforcing framework within the specimens, effectively changing the failure mode from brittle to ductile39.

sec.title: Range analysis
sec.p: The compressive strength test results of the specimens under various factors (CSA content, S/B, and W/B) were intuitively analyzed, as presented in Table 6. The range of compressive strength for each factor at different ages is denoted by R. Statistics k1, k2, k3, and k4 were utilized in the intuitive analysis to represent the average compressive strength of each factor at distinct levels. Through standardizing the range values of each factor, the proportional distribution of the ranges across different indicators was computed. When considering the 7-day compressive strength as the indicator, the proportions of the range for each factor are as follows: RA is 27.47%, RB is 28.89%, and RC is 43.64%. The primary and secondary influencing factors are determined as follows: when C > B> A, the W/B ratio exhibits the largest range value and the highest influence proportion. This suggests that during the initial stages of concrete formation, the W/B ratio significantly impacts the strength development of concrete materials, while the S/B ratio and CSA dosage play relatively minor roles, although their influences are comparable.When the 28-day compressive strength was employed as an indicator, the proportions of each factor’s range were as follows: RA was 32.17%, RB was 48.94%, and RC was 18.89%. The primary and secondary influencing factors were identified as B > A > C. At this stage, the range of the sand/binder ratio exhibited the largest variation, and its influence was also the most pronounced. Additionally, the range of CSA increased, along with its corresponding influence. These findings suggest that during the later curing period (28 days), the two parameters—S/B ratio and CSA substitution rate—significantly affect the growth of the material’s compressive strength.

caption.p: .Range analysis results of multiple factors affecting the UCS of CHSC.

sec.title: Destructive form
sec.p: To gain a deeper understanding of the compression failure mechanisms in the specimens, three groups of samples with 28-day compressive strengths exceeding 90 MPa were selected for detailed analysis. Figure 5 presents images illustrating the compression failure modes of cubic specimens numbered 1, 9, and 15 at both 7 and 28 days. The specimens exhibit characteristic plastic failure behavior, evidenced by complex crack patterns with varying crack widths—some cracks are notably wide, while others remain narrow. A prominent, large-scale crack consistently extends along the specimen’s edge from top to bottom, accompanied by multiple smaller cracks distributed elsewhere. Crack initiation typically occurs at the corners of the cubic specimens and progressively propagates downward.

The loading process leading to failure resembles the yielding behavior observed in steel reinforcement, progressing sequentially through distinct stages: an initial elastic-like phase, a primary yielding phase, a strengthening phase, and, ultimately, a failure phase. This progression suggests that the incorporation of steel fibers alters the inherent failure mode of the concrete. After the onset of initial failure, the steel fibers actively contribute to load-bearing by establishing tensile bonds between the cementitious matrix and the aggregates. This interaction facilitates the redistribution of internal stresses within the specimen, enabling it to sustain loads beyond the initial failure point until ultimate failure occurs.

caption.p: Compression failure modes of cubic specimen of 7-day and 28-day.

sec.p: During the intermediate to final stages of loading, an audible noise is emitted just before specimen failure. This phenomenon indicates that the steel fibers, which act as a binding agent, have been pulled out and detached from the aggregates and the cementitious matrix. As a result, the fibers lose their cooperative load-bearing capacity, leading to the specimen’s failure.

sec.title: SEM–EDS testing
sec.p: To investigate the internal microstructure and the evolution of hydration products in CHSC under varying curing agent (CA) conditions, we conducted microscopic morphological analyses on CHSC specimens cured for different durations (7 and 28 days) using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Figure 6 presents the microstructural characteristics of CHSC samples at these two curing ages. Subsequent image processing with ImageJ software allowed for clear visualization of pore size distributions within the CHSC specimens at each curing interval, as shown in Fig. 6(a) and 6(b), with quantitative data provided in Fig. 6(c) and 6(d). Furthermore, Fig. 7 displays energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) elemental distribution maps of CHSC surfaces subjected to various CA conditions at 500× magnification, while Table 7 summarizes the corresponding mass fractions of detected elements obtained from surface scanning.

caption.p: Processing images of micro-morphology (MS) and pore structure (PS) of CHSC sample (500×) at different CA: (a) MS-7d, (b) MS-28d, (c) PS-7d, (d) PS-28d.

sec.p: According to Fig. 6, when the curing period is 7 days, the surface of the CHSC samples shows numerous and large pores, with a relatively loose internal structure and a low degree of hydration. The scanning electron microscope images reveal an uneven surface morphology. As the curing time increases, the internal hydration of the CHSC continues, and the hydration products gradually fill the internal pores. The number of visible pores on the sample surface decreases, and the internal structure becomes relatively denser, with the SEM images showing a smoother surface. By estimating the proportion of the pore areas, the porosity of the CHSC samples at different curing times can be determined. The internal porosity of CHSC at 7 days and 28 days is 23.5% and 6.62%, respectively, which is consistent with the strength test results (CHSC strength increases with longer curing periods).

caption.p: Distribution of EDS surface scanning elements in the microstructure of CHSC samples at different CA (500 ×): (a) 7 d, (b) 28d.

sec.p: The analytical results presented in Figs. 6 and 7, and Table 7 indicate that the primary hydration products within the samples were C-S-H gel, AFt, and Ca(OH)2, along with a notable formation of CaCO3. Micromorphological analysis at 500x magnification, as shown in Fig. 6, demonstrated that during the early curing stage (7 days), the CHSC exhibited a greater number of larger pores and a reduced quantity of hydration products. As the content of CA increased, the formation of hydration products within the CHSC persisted. The number and size of these hydration products increased, leading to their interconnection and the subsequent filling of internal pores in the CHSC, which further enhanced the material’s internal densification. By the time the curing period reached 28 days (Fig. 6(b)), the pores were substantially filled with a considerable amount of C-S-H in a flocculated form, resulting in further internal densification of the material. Consequently, the strength of the material also improved significantly.This finding aligns with the results of the orthogonal analysis, which indicate that an increased sand-to-cement ratio and higher CSA content at 28 days contribute to enhanced strength performance. The calcium-to-silicon (Ca/Si) ratio of the CHSC material was measured at 19.34:14.99 at 7 days and 19.73:14.48 at 28 days, demonstrating an increase in the Ca/Si ratio over time. Correspondingly, the material’s strength further improves as the curing time progresses.

caption.p: Mass fractions of elements after EDS surface scanning (%).

sec.title: XRD testing
sec.p: The hydration products of CHSC were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) to assess the influence of CA on these products. Figure 8 presents the XRD spectra of CHSC at various CA values. The data indicate that hydration products, including aqueous magnesite (AFt), Ca(OH)2, C-S-H, CaCO3, and lawsonite, were present in samples from both ages. Additionally, numerous characteristic peaks of quartz were identified in the raw material. The characteristic peaks of quartz(2θ = 26°) and the Ca(OH)2 (2θ = 29°)phase diminished with increasing CA, suggesting that SiO2, the primary active component in CSA, continuously consumed Ca(OH)2, the initial hydration product, leading to the formation of new C-S-H(2θ = 41°~51°). The sample with a CA of 28 days exhibited many characteristic peaks of AFt and C-S-H compared to the sample with a CA of 7 days, with the original peaks continuing to intensify. This observation indicates a sustained increase in the quantity of hydration products such as AFt(Ettringite), C-S-H, and lawsonite. Furthermore, it suggests that SiO2 in CSA possesses significant reactivity, actively participating in the secondary hydration process of the material and generating a substantial amount of additional hydration products, which ultimately enhances the strength of the samples.

caption.p: XRD patterns of CHSC at different CA: (a)7 days; (b)28 days.

sec.title: Discussion of environmental benefits
sec.p: The environmental impact associated with concrete production primarily stems from carbon emissions related to the manufacturing of cementitious materials and the transportation of aggregates. This study focuses mainly on the variations in carbon emissions resulting from the production of cementitious materials. The environmental costs associated with producing these materials comprise four main components: fuel consumption (thermal energy), greenhouse gas emissions, electricity usage, and transportation expenses48–50. Solid waste materials, such as silica fume, are excluded from recalculations in this analysis because they are industrial or agricultural by-products; therefore, only the costs related to their collection and post-processing are considered. In this study, corn stalk ash and silica fume were used as partial replacements for cement, achieving a substitution rate of 20% by mass. Corn stalk ash was produced by calcination at 500 °C for one hour, with the primary difference in carbon emissions attributed to electricity consumption during this process. The carbon emission value for the optimal mix, designated C12, is presented in Table 8. Compared to the carbon emission value of 931 kg per ton for conventional cement, this material demonstrates an emission reduction of 18.69%.

caption.p: Material carbon emission factor (kg CO2-e/kg) and concrete carbon emission (kg CO2-e/m3).

list-item.p: Experimental findings demonstrated that incorporating a blend of calcium sulfoaluminate (CSA) and silica fume (SF) as supplementary cementitious materials enables the production of biomass ash-based high-strength concrete with favorable properties. In this investigation, approximately 37.5% of the mix designs produced CHSC specimens with 28-day compressive strengths exceeding 80 MPa.

The analysis identified three principal factors influencing the compressive strength of CHSC: the sand-to-binder (S/B) ratio, CSA content, and water-to-binder (W/B) ratio, listed in descending order of impact. Optimal conditions were observed with a CSA content of 15%, a W/B ratio of 0.21, and an S/B ratio of 1.2, resulting in a maximum 28-day compressive strength of 109.7 MPa. Conversely, a CSA content of 5%, a W/B ratio of 0.9, and an S/B ratio of 0.18 yielded a peak 7-day strength of 79.1 MPa. Notably, using CSA as the sole supplementary cementitious material did not produce satisfactory strength outcomes in CHSC.

Microstructural analyses of the highest-strength CHSC specimens revealed a progressive formation of hydration products with increasing curing age. The elevated reactive SiO2 content in CSA facilitated secondary hydration reactions, resulting in the generation of substantial amounts of additional calcium silicate hydrate (C-S-H) gel. This process contributed to the densification of the concrete’s internal microstructure, thereby enhancing its mechanical strength.

The use of a combined CSA and SF system as auxiliary cementitious materials in CHSC production was found to reduce carbon dioxide emissions by approximately 15%, thereby advancing the construction industry’s progress toward carbon neutrality.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: Conceptualization, methodology, data curation, investigation, writing—original draft, writing—review draft and editing, R.W.; methodology, validation, resources, investigation, G.W.; supervision, methodology, validation, Y.C.; investigation, validation, Y.W.and X.X. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its Supplementary Information.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
